I'm not sure this will always be the correct action to take. I've been working through some confusion with the Thermostat API with Vera support, and I'm still a bit confused TBH. Here are some quotes from our support thread:

> Auto is not a mode which you should set up manually, would be better if you could set your Heat and Cool setpoints and then leave Auto to work on it’s own making the average and running on that value.

> Also, in order to work properly, for some thermostats the different between Heat and Cool must be greater than 3 degrees, please try to enlarge the gap between Heat and Cool and then give Auto a try.

In a non-auto mode, setting `CurrentSetpoint` seems like the appropriate action to take. When a thermostat is in Auto mode, it almost sounds like they want the following actions to take place to make a change:

* Set the operating mode to some non-auto value
* Update the heat and cool values, making sure they're least 3 degrees apart
* Set the operating mode back to the existing mode

So, in short, this isn't right, but it's clear to me that I need to do some more testing before I'm able to tell you what to change it to.

```
q = aioxmpp.ibr.xso.Query()
q.username = ""
q.password = ""
print([
    tag
    for tag, descriptor in q.CHILD_MAP.items()
    if descriptor.__get__(q, type(q)) is not None
])
```

I think I'd prefer to log when these errors occur, just in case, so that it doesn't silently cause something else to fail in some edge case in the future. I'm happy to make that change myself after merging your patch.
@justinsacbibit I'm not sure we need this test.

For other errors, the logging framework is used as well even if the logged message could not be sent directly (e.g. on network errors). But using the logging framework enables other handlers as well to be fired (e.g. a mail handler, a stderr handler or anything else). So even if we are unable to send our events, other handlers might inform the user about the problem.

We have `LogProcessingWorker._safe_log` to more or less safely log a message: use the logging framework except we are in the process of a shutdown, then just print to `stderr`.
We could move this method into `utils.py` to make it a more easy accesible function.

In general, I don't like basic `stderr` (or `stdout`) logging very much. I often work on projects where there is no `stderr`/`stdout` because the application is a daemon. IMO it should be just a last resort if everything else failed.

Aah, I should have probably have seen this, since in fact ATMO had the same problems of having the schedule tasks dynamically and in the end I used [redbeat](https://github.com/sibson/redbeat) for that (https://github.com/mozilla/telemetry-analysis-service/pull/318 and https://github.com/mozilla/telemetry-analysis-service/pull/379), which is a similar thing like the old, now defunct django-celery package. It basically provides a custom scheduler that persists the periodic task list in Redis and a bit of API to add/remove it after startup.

That said I don't think the use cases are the same and if we can solve this with the MUCH smaller solution of yours let's do it.

In Flask the best practice for extensions is to have them [register themeselves](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/1.0/api/#flask.Flask.extensions). So I would suggest to change the code in https://github.com/mozilla/redash/pull/444/files#diff-ad93995bb355251c02f87fc02db69351R82 to not populate the list there but do it in the extension itself, e.g.::

```python
def datasource_health(app):
    """A Redash extension to add datasource health status reporting."""

    # Override the default status API view with our extended view
    app.view_functions['%s.status_api' % routes.name] = stmo_status_api
 
    # Add the update_health_status task to a list of periodic tasks
    if not hasattr(app, 'periodic_tasks'):
        app.periodic_tasks = {}

    app.periodic_tasks['update_health_status'] = {
        'signature': update_health_status.s(),
        'schedule': settings.HEALTH_QUERIES_REFRESH_SCHEDULE,
    }
```

As you can see, instead of inventing our own return format, I've just used a dictionary of keyword parameters that [add_periodic_task expects](https://github.com/celery/celery/blob/eeda18611ceed2560145f95ada4977a1b825d282/celery/app/base.py#L974-L975) so we keep the design open for any kind of tasks that Celery supports and other Redash extensions may need in the future.

The use of `.s()` for the signature parameter is also significant since it'll allow Celery to store information about a task in its task store as metadata instead of as Python callables, which are hard to serialize for example to JSON (the recently new default in Celery). In other words, this will be a great help to stay consistent with Celery API design and by that reduce risk for using tasks like this.

To test this function, we need to mock requests. If you wanna give it a try, take a look how to use the `responses` library in tests: https://github.com/getsentry/responses#response-body-as-string

Then you would call `get_mime_type` with the URL you have mocked and you test to make sure the function is doing the correct things. You can then test several things, such as:

* When `Content-Type` is given (this you can mock as part of responses.add), assert that the function returns it
* When `Content-Type` is not given, assert that an exception is raised. (https://pytest.org/latest/assert.html#assertions-about-expected-exceptions)
* When `url` is an empty string ("") or None, assert that an empty string is returned.

`auto()`  is only available in 3.6 and the enum backport. I used the solution given in the 3.5 documentation:
```python
class AutoNumber(Enum):
    def __new__(cls):
        value = len(cls.__members__) + 1
        obj = object.__new__(cls)
        obj._value_ = value
        return obj


class ParameterUsageOptions(AutoNumber):
    '''Different usage options for parameter lists.'''

    SESSION_METHOD_DECLARATION = ()
    '''For declaring a regular method in Session'''
    SESSION_INIT_DECLARATION = ()```

Because placing it here (or even as default in the register_cli_argument) results in the validator always failing because for N/A storage profiles, this has a value.  There are a few ways to handle this:

1) have the validators inspect the namespace as it was provided and before defaults were applied. This is what I have always preferred but it would require infrastructure changes and got shot down.

2) Apply the "MarkSpecifiedAction" to these parameters. This appends "__SET__" to the value when it is supplied by the user but not when it is defaulted, which permits you to distinguish between the a default value and the user specifying the same value as the default. However, this has proven to introduce bugs and is basically a hacky workaround, so I don't want to do that. (I tried the implementation, it was messy, I removed it).

3) Make these None and then apply the default in-code and in the help text. While not ideal, it's the simplest fix. This is also the official guidance if a complex type is the default (for example a default dictionary or list) so it's not totally contrary to guidance.

4) Keep the defaults and don't validate. This means that if the user specifies a value that is N/A, it will be silently ignored. We've gone to great lengths to throw errors when users specify invalid parameter combinations, so, especially for our most complex command, this didn't seem like something to let slide.

Given those 4 options, I really prefer 1, but I' okay with (and chose) 3.

@TomAugspurger you're right about `sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV` not working well with scalar dask arrays as a score. The example snippet below is fine when `compute=True`, but fails when `compute=False`

```python
from functools import partial

from dask_ml.datasets import make_classification
from dask_ml.wrappers import Incremental
from dask_ml.metrics import accuracy_score

from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer
from sklearn.linear_model import SGDClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Get dataset
X, y = make_classification(chunks=25)

estimator = SGDClassifier(random_state=10)
acc = partial(accuracy_score, compute=False)
acc.__name__ = 'accuracy_score' # make_scorer looks for __name__
clf = Incremental(estimator, scoring=make_scorer(acc))

gs = GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid={'estimator__loss': ['hinge', 'log']})
gs.fit(X, y, classes=[0, 1])
```

When `compute=False`, calling `GridSearchCV.fit` raises 

```
ValueError: scoring must return a number, got dask.array<mul, shape=(), dtype=float64, chunksize=()> (<class 'dask.array.core.Array'>) instead. (scorer=score)
```

which comes from https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/30ebb98dff5a7773ac66d068c8103cd47c0427ec/sklearn/model_selection/_validation.py#L606-L609

More `argparse` fun! As documented [here](https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/argparse.html#default), if the default is a `str` and you specify a `type` argument when adding the CLI flag to the parser, the `type` parameter gets called with the default before adding it to the namespace. As a result, sometimes when `type` was called we were just parsing the default rather than a user provided value so we had to use our `set_by_cli()` function to differentiate between these cases.

This avoids all that because the `action` argument is only called if the user provides a value.

Making this change doesn't break anything. It's certainly not a necessary change, but it should be a little faster.

You probably have used generators without even knowing it! `range` returns a list. `xrange` returns a generator. No real gotchas. Closest thing to a gotcha is you can only iterate forward through generator expressions and you can only go through them once. None of that matters here.

Suppose a user has multiple vhosts in a file and he runs Certbot for only one of those domains. If he runs Certbot again for another domain, we'll currently blow away the configuration file created during his first run. I think this behavior kind of sucks and we can easily fix it.

I think we should either always use `a` (which should create the file if it doesn't exist) or append a number to the filename when necessary like `-le-ssl-2.conf` to avoid overwriting. In most situations, I think using `a` for the mode is fine, but it can certainly go wrong. The easy example is the user has a matching `-le-ssl.conf` file but it's invalid and is disabled. Here we'll add our own directives, enable it, Apache will fail, and we'll have to revert our changes.

def setUp(self):

```

Here is one way to make it work:
* ONNX ML has a separate IR version number
* If a BC-breaking change is made in the ONNX ML fragment of the proto language, only the ONNX ML IR version number is bumped
* If a BC-breaking change is made in the ONNX fragment, the ONNX IR and ONNX ML IR version numbers are bumped.

Thus, ONNX only clients don't see any IR version changes from ONNX ML, and ONNX ML clients see a coherent version number profile by looking at the ONNX ML number only.
